title: Unblocker: Nudge
description: Generates a concrete action to break through a writing block.
entries: Skip the scene you are dreading and write the one right after it.
Kill the character you have been protecting.
Rewrite the last paragraph from a different character's eyes.
Have someone lie, and do not let the reader know yet.
Take the thing your protagonist wants most and give it to them too soon.
Cut the first three paragraphs and start where it actually gets interesting.
Let a character say the one thing everyone has been avoiding.
Set a timer for ten minutes and write the worst possible version on purpose.
Interrupt the scene with bad news that has nothing to do with the plot.
Give your calmest character a reason to lose their temper.
Delete every adverb on the page and see what the sentence still needs.
Move the scene outdoors and let the weather push back.
Ask what would happen if the plan actually worked, and write past it.
Introduce a stranger who wants something small and inconvenient.
Write the argument the characters are too polite to have.
Take away your protagonist's best option and see what they reach for next.
Change the time of day and rewrite the same beat in the new light.
Let a minor character walk off with the scene for one page.
Have your protagonist do the generous thing for a selfish reason.
Burn down the setting; make them finish the scene without it.
Write the flashback you keep gesturing at, then decide if you need it.
Give two characters the same goal and only one way to reach it.
Say the theme out loud in one plain sentence, then hide it back in the scene.
Make the reliable character wrong about something important.
Cut the dialogue tags and let the voices carry themselves.
Start the chapter in the middle of an action, no throat-clearing.
Have a character keep a promise at the worst possible moment.
Write the letter your protagonist would never dare send.
Take your favorite line and delete it; the scene may have been leaning on it.
Put a clock on the scene: something has to happen before something else does.
Let a character notice the detail you, the writer, keep noticing.
Have the help arrive, and let it make everything worse.
Ask what your antagonist would say they are the hero of, and write that page.
Give your protagonist a small, embarrassing want alongside the large noble one.
Trade the confession for a gesture; show it instead of saying it.
Write the scene as a phone call so you cannot lean on description.
Take the safest choice off the table and force a real decision.
Let someone be kind at a moment when cruelty would be easier.
Zoom in on one object and let the whole scene turn around it.
End the chapter one paragraph earlier than feels comfortable.
